facts.fact: In a balanced binary search tree, search/insert/delete operations are O(log n). In an unbalanced tree where elements are added in sorted order, the tree degenerates into a linked list with O(n) complexity.
AVL trees have maximum height of approximately 1.44*log(n), while Red-Black trees are bounded by 2*log(n). AVL trees are 0.947x the speed of red-black trees on average (median across 79 test runs).
For a binary heap, insertion and removal take O(log n) time. Building a heap from n elements takes O(n) amortized time due to the build_maxheap function running only n/2 times with decreasing costs.
Fibonacci heaps achieve O(1) amortized time for insert and find-minimum, with O(log n) amortized for decrease-key and delete operations.
Trie search, insertion, and deletion operations have O(L) time complexity where L is the length of the string, not O(log n) like BSTs.

Hash table load factor of 0.75 is the standard Java HashMap threshold. Exceeding this threshold triggers rehashing (doubling capacity). Low load factor wastes memory; high load factor increases collision chains.
Double hashing and binary probing outperform other collision resolution techniques in high-load environments, delivering more uniform key distribution.
Dynamic array append using doubling strategy has O(1) amortized cost. Total cost for m appends is m (appends) + 2m (doubling) = 3m, which is O(m) amortized.
Converting recursive memoization to bottom-up DP eliminates O(n) call stack space, keeping only O(k) where k is the number of dependencies. For Fibonacci, this reduces space from O(n) to O(1).
Binary search applies to any monotonic function, not just sorted arrays. The search completes in at most O(log N) evaluations regardless of domain.
Parametric search transforms a decision algorithm (threshold test) into an optimization algorithm. Binary search is simpler in practice, often preferred over parametric search.
Heap sort achieves O(n log n) time in all cases (best, average, worst) with O(1) space. It's faster than selection sort's O(n²) by replacing O(n) max-finding with O(log n) heap operations.
Netflix uses max heaps for ranking top-k recommendations. Over 80% of Netflix viewing originates from recommendations, making heap efficiency critical for latency.
Google search autocomplete stores prefixes in a trie with frequency counts at each node. To show top-k results efficiently, nodes store hit counts rather than requiring full trie scans.
LRU cache combines a hash table and doubly-linked list for O(1) get/put operations. Nodes are moved to head on access; tail node is evicted when capacity exceeded.
B-Tree range queries outperform hash indexes by orders of magnitude. They maintain sorted leaf nodes connected linearly, enabling sequential range scans after the starting point is located.

Aho-Corasick algorithm finds all occurrences of a dictionary of strings in text in O(n + m + z) time, where n is text length, m is total dictionary size, z is number of matches. Suffix trees cannot efficiently solve substring dictionary problems.

Binary search on answer is effective for optimization problems with constraints too large for brute force. If you can frame a minimization/maximization as a YES/NO feasibility question with monotonic answers, binary search applies.
Red-Black trees require fewer rotations during insertion/deletion (amortized constant) compared to AVL trees, making them preferred in C++ STL, Java TreeMap, and Linux kernel scheduler.
When building a heap from n unsorted elements, the bottom-up heapify approach is O(n), not O(n log n), because most nodes are leaves with zero heapify cost.

examples.key_insight: Stores top-k most-searched prefixes at each node rather than scanning terabytes of data for every autocomplete suggestion.
Heaps enable O(log n) top-k selection from candidate recommendations, critical for sub-100ms latency in streaming UI.
Load factor 0.75 triggers rehashing. Double hashing outperforms alternatives in high-contention scenarios.

examples.scale: Standard library default for ordered maps
examples.key_insight: Red-Black trees used instead of AVL due to fewer rotations on frequent insertions/deletions, despite slightly taller bounds.

examples.scenario: Database range queries (e.g., SELECT * WHERE date BETWEEN)
examples.key_insight: Leaf node pointers enable sequential scanning after binary search to starting point; hash indexes unable to support range queries.

examples.scenario: LRU Cache in CPU / Memory hierarchy
examples.scale: L1-L3 cache eviction, buffer pool management
examples.key_insight: O(1) get/put/evict enables cache replacement at CPU clock speeds. Alternative single-structure approaches require O(log n) or worse.
O(n + m + z) single-pass scan over text finds all occurrences of all dictionary patterns. Trie variants require per-pattern traversals.

examples.scenario: Allocating tasks to minimize max load
examples.scale: k workers, n tasks with variable duration
examples.key_insight: Reframe as monotonic feasibility: 'Can we assign all tasks so max load ≤ X?'. Answer space is 1D; binary search reduces O(n*k) brute force to O(log(max_duration) * n).

analogies.analogy: A BST is a telephone directory (sorted, supports range queries). A heap is a triage queue at a hospital (quick access to highest-priority only, no ordering of rest).

A parking lot: load factor is cars/spaces. At 0.3, plenty of empty spaces (memory wasted). At 0.75, manageable. At 0.95, cars circling for spots (collision chains). At 1.0+, overflow lot required (rehashing).

A BST is alphabetical order (like a dictionary index by word). A trie is a filing cabinet where each drawer level represents a letter position (like autocomplete guides you left-to-right through letters).

Memoization is cooking with a sous vide: you precook components and cache them, but still assemble top-down (high recursion overhead). Bottom-up DP is assembly-line manufacturing: assemble base units first, then combine upward (no recursion cost).

Dynamic array doubling is like renting a studio (small lease), then moving to a 2x-size apartment (one big move). The rent grows infrequently, but you amortize the moving cost over all the months you stay, making per-month cost constant.

You're searching for the maximum weight a bridge can hold: you don't test every weight incrementally. Instead, test 500kg (no), then 750kg (yes), then 625kg (no)—binary search converges in log steps. The 'search space' is kg, not indices.

AVL trees are obsessive perfectionists (rebalance constantly, strict height bounds). Red-Black trees are pragmatists (accept slightly imbalance, fewer rebalances overall). AVL reads faster; Red-Black writes faster.
Linked list is a waitlist (MRU at front, LRU at back). Hash table is a VIP pass (instant lookup who's in the list). Together, O(1) cut-to-front and O(1) evict-from-back.

Heapify costs decrease exponentially from leaves upward; amortized O(1) per node.

key_numbers.value: O(n + m + z) where n=text, m=dictionary size, z=matches

key_numbers.context: Hash lookup + linked-list pointer manipulation, both constant.
Leaf node sequential pointers eliminate sorting overhead.
Of all viewing comes from recommendations, driving heap/priority-queue optimization ROI.

Guaranteed, unlike quicksort. Space O(1) in-place.

key_numbers.context: Heap enables efficient max-finding per selection iteration.

common_misconceptions.misconception: BSTs always have O(log n) search.
common_misconceptions.reality: Unbalanced trees degenerate to O(n). Inserting sorted data into a naive BST creates a linked list. Use self-balancing variants (AVL, Red-Black) or randomized trees (Treaps) for guaranteed log bounds.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash tables have O(1) search guaranteed.
common_misconceptions.reality: O(1) is amortized. Worst case is O(n) if all keys hash to the same bucket. Exceeding load factor (0.75) triggers costly rehashing. Hash function quality matters; poor hash functions ruin performance.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tries are always faster than BSTs for strings.
common_misconceptions.reality: Tries are O(L) per operation (L = string length). BSTs are O(log n * L) (log n comparisons, each L-char comparison). For very long strings, BSTs with string interning can win. For prefixes and autocomplete, tries excel.
Amortized means average over sequence of operations. Individual ops can be expensive (dynamic array append worst-case O(n) when doubling). Affects latency-sensitive code—some ops will spike.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Memoization and bottom-up DP have same space complexity.
common_misconceptions.reality: Memoization requires O(n) cache + O(recursion depth) stack. Bottom-up DP can often reduce to O(k) where k = number of dependencies. For Fibonacci, memoization is O(n); bottom-up is O(1).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search only works on sorted arrays.
common_misconceptions.reality: Binary search works on any monotonic function with integer domain. Parametric search, binary search on answer (min allocation, max load), and other non-array searches are equally valid.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Red-Black trees are slower than AVL trees.
common_misconceptions.reality: Red-Black trees are faster on write-heavy workloads (fewer rotations). AVL is faster on read-heavy. Empirically, Red-Black ≈ AVL on mixed workloads; library default suggests write-heavy dominates.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Load factor 1.0 is fine for hash tables.
common_misconceptions.reality: At load factor 1.0, collision chains grow unbounded; expected lookup O(n). Standard threshold is 0.75. Beyond that, rehashing is mandatory to maintain O(1) amortized.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Heaps must maintain full sorted order.
common_misconceptions.reality: Heaps only maintain heap property (parent ≥ children for max-heap), not full sort. This is why heap-sort is O(n log n)—only O(log n) per extraction, not O(n) per full sort.

Trie space is O(ALPHABET_SIZE * number_of_nodes), which can be O(m) where m is total characters in all strings stored. Dense tries with full alphabet (26-256) per node use more space than BSTs for small dictionaries.

common_misconceptions.misconception: LRU cache with only HashMap is O(1).
common_misconceptions.reality: HashMap alone cannot achieve O(1) eviction (requires tracking access order). Double-linked list is mandatory for O(1) reordering. Hash only is insufficient.

common_misconceptions.misconception: B-Trees are just BSTs with more children.
common_misconceptions.reality: B-Trees optimize for disk I/O (cache-line and block-aware). Wide branching reduces tree height; leaf pointers enable range queries. BSTs optimize for RAM. Different constraints, different designs.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Suffix trees are always better than tries for string problems.
common_misconceptions.reality: Suffix trees are powerful for single-text pattern matching (find all occurrences of pattern in one text). For multi-pattern matching (dictionary in text), Aho-Corasick automaton (trie-based) is faster and simpler.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Parametric search is always better than binary search on answer.
common_misconceptions.reality: Binary search on answer is empirically faster and simpler. Parametric search finds strongly polynomial algorithms in rare cases, but engineering effort and constant factors make binary search practical choice.
Doubling uses at most 2x peak memory during single doubling operation. Amortized space is 2x (e.g., vector capacity is ≤ 2 * size). Trade-off is worth O(1) amortized append.
